preamble.summary: Asaph's crisis of faith. The psalmist confesses that he nearly lost his footing when he saw the prosperity of the wicked. Their lives appear effortless, free of suffering, draped in arrogance — and they mock God with impunity. The psalmist's own faithfulness seems pointless until he enters the sanctuary and perceives the wicked's final destiny. The psalm pivots from envy to trust, culminating in one of the most intimate declarations of faith in the Hebrew Bible: 'Whom have I in heaven but You?'
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 73 is the Hebrew Bible's most honest exploration of theodicy at the personal level. Unlike Job, who suffers catastrophically, the psalmist here suffers the quieter torment of watching the wicked thrive while he remains faithful for apparently no return. His near-apostasy is described with startling physical imagery — 'my feet had almost slipped, my steps had nearly given way' (verse 2). What saves him is not an intellectual answer but an encounter in the sanctuary (verse 17) where he perceives — the verb is not 'understands' but 'perceives,' an act of spiritual seeing rather than rational calculation. The resolution in verses 23-28 is not that suffering is explained but that God's presence is sufficient. The psalm moves from 'Why do the wicked prosper?' to 'You are enough.'
preamble.friction: The phrase akh tov le-Yisrael Elohim ('Surely God is good to Israel') in verse 1 has a textual crux. The Masoretic text reads le-Yisrael ('to Israel'), but the parallel with levvar levav ('the pure in heart') has led many to read la-yashar ('to the upright') — a one-letter emendation. We retain the Masoretic reading, since the tension between communal confession ('God is good to Israel') and personal crisis ('but as for me, my feet almost slipped') is precisely the psalm's point.
preamble.connections: Psalm 73 opens Book III of the Psalter (Psalms 73-89), a collection dominated by Asaph psalms and national laments. Its placement at the head of Book III is deliberate: after the royal optimism of Psalm 72 (closing Book II), Psalm 73 begins with doubt. The psalm is often compared to Job and Ecclesiastes as wisdom literature wrestling with the same question: does righteousness pay? The answer here is distinctive — not that it pays in material terms, but that the relationship itself is the reward. Verse 25 ('Whom have I in heaven but You?') anticipates Habakkuk 3:17-18, where the prophet rejoices in God even when the fig tree does not blossom.

verses.text_hebrew: מִזְמ֗וֹר לְאָ֫סָ֥ף אַ֤ךְ ט֭וֹב לְיִשְׂרָאֵ֥ל אֱלֹהִ֗ים לְבָרֵ֥י לֵבָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Truly God is good to Israel, even to such as are of a clean heart.
verses.rendering:
A psalm of Asaph.

Surely God is good to Israel,
to those pure in heart.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm begins with a conclusion — akh tov ('surely good') — that the rest of the psalm will nearly dismantle before rebuilding on different foundations. This opening is the orthodox position: God is good to Israel, to the pure in heart. The psalmist states the doctrine first, then confesses how close he came to abandoning it. The word akh ('surely, only, indeed') is emphatic but also limiting — it can carry the nuance of 'only,' hinting that this goodness is not as obvious as it should be.
translator_notes: The Masoretic text reads le-Yisrael ('to Israel'). Many scholars emend to la-yashar ('to the upright') based on the parallel with levvar levav ('pure of heart'). Both readings are theologically coherent: the MT emphasizes God's goodness to the covenant people, while the emendation emphasizes God's goodness to the morally upright.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִ֗י כִּ֭מְעַט נָטָ֣יוּ רַגְלָ֑י כְּ֝אַ֗יִן שֻׁפְּכ֥וּ אֲשֻׁרָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: But as for me, my feet were almost gone; my steps had well nigh slipped.
verses.rendering:
But as for me — my feet had almost stumbled;
my steps had nearly slipped away.
verses.expanded_rendering: The adversative va-ani ('but as for me') is the hinge of the psalm. The doctrine says God is good; the psalmist's experience says otherwise. The verbs are physical and visceral: natyu raglai ('my feet turned aside, deviated') and shuppekhu ashurai ('my steps were poured out, spilled'). The image is of someone on a steep path whose feet slide out from under them — not a deliberate decision to leave the faith but an involuntary loss of footing. The psalmist did not choose to doubt; doubt happened to him.
translator_notes: The verb shuppekhu ('were poured out') is striking — it describes liquid being spilled, steps dissolving like water. The near-apostasy is depicted not as rebellion but as erosion: the ground beneath the psalmist's convictions was washing away.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־קִ֭נֵּאתִי בַּהוֹלְלִ֑ים שְׁל֖וֹם רְשָׁעִ֣ים אֶרְאֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: For I was envious at the foolish, when I saw the prosperity of the wicked.
verses.rendering:
For I envied the arrogant
when I saw the well-being of the wicked.
verses.expanded_rendering: The cause of the crisis is named plainly: qinneiti ('I was envious'). The psalmist watched the hollelim ('arrogant, boastful ones') and saw their shalom ('peace, wholeness, prosperity') — the very thing that should belong to the righteous. The word shalom is loaded: it is the covenant blessing (Numbers 6:26), the fruit of obedience. When the wicked possess shalom and the righteous do not, the covenant itself appears broken.
translator_notes: The word hollelim ('arrogant, boastful') comes from halal in its negative sense — 'to be foolish, to boast.' These are not merely wicked people but people who flaunt their wickedness. Their arrogance is part of what makes them unbearable to the psalmist.

key_terms.semantic_range: peace, wholeness, completeness, welfare, prosperity, health, safety
key_terms.note: Shalom is not merely the absence of conflict but the presence of complete well-being — physical, social, economic, spiritual. When the wicked possess shalom, the covenant's promise-and-reward structure appears to have failed.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י אֵ֖ין חַרְצֻבּ֥וֹת לְמוֹתָ֗ם וּבָרִ֥יא אוּלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: For there are no bands in their death: but their strength is firm.
verses.rendering:
For they have no pain in their death;
their bodies are sleek and well-fed.
translator_notes: The word chartsubot ('bonds, pangs, fetters') is debated. Some read it as 'pangs' (they suffer no pain), others as 'bonds' (nothing binds or constrains them). The following phrase uvari ulam ('and fat/healthy is their body') supports the reading that these are people whose physical condition betrays no suffering. They are visibly prosperous — their ease is written on their bodies.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּעֲמַ֣ל אֱנ֣וֹשׁ אֵינֵ֑מוֹ וְעִם־אָ֝דָ֗ם לֹ֣א יְנֻגָּֽעוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They are not in trouble as other men; neither are they plagued like other men.
verses.rendering:
They are not burdened like other mortals;
they are not afflicted like other people.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist observes that the wicked seem exempt from the universal human condition. The word amal ('toil, trouble, misery') is the lot of every human being since Genesis 3, yet these people escape it. The phrase im adam ('with humankind') emphasizes their apparent separation from the common experience of suffering. They live as if the curse does not apply to them.
translator_notes: The parallel between enosh ('mortal man' — emphasizing frailty) and adam ('humankind') creates a comprehensive statement: the wicked are exempt from the suffering that defines human existence. This is what makes them so maddening to the psalmist.

verses.text_hebrew: לָ֭כֵן עֲנָקַ֣תְמוֹ גַאֲוָ֑ה יַעֲטָף־שִׁ֝֗ית חָמָ֥ס לָֽמוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore pride compasseth them about as a chain; violence covereth them as a garment.
verses.rendering:
Therefore pride is their necklace;
violence wraps them like a garment.
verses.expanded_rendering: Because they suffer no consequences, pride becomes their adornment. The word anaqah ('necklace, chain') transforms arrogance into jewelry — they wear their pride as decoration. Violence (chamas) is their clothing — it covers them completely, it is what they are dressed in, it defines their public identity. The imagery is of people who have turned vice into fashion.
translator_notes: The word chamas ('violence, wrongdoing') is the same word used in Genesis 6:11 to describe the pre-flood world that provoked God's destruction. Its use here foreshadows the eventual judgment described later in the psalm.

verses.text_hebrew: יָ֭צָא מֵחֵ֣לֶב עֵינֵ֑מוֹ עָ֝בְר֗וּ מַשְׂכִּיּ֥וֹת לֵבָֽב׃
verses.text_kjv: Their eyes stand out with fatness: they have more than heart could wish.
verses.rendering:
Their eyes bulge out from fatness;
the fantasies of their hearts overflow.
translator_notes: The image is grotesque: eyes protruding from fat faces — physical excess mirroring moral excess. The phrase avru maskiyyot levav ('the imaginations of the heart overflow, pass beyond') suggests that their desires know no limit — they have everything and still want more. The word maskiyyot can mean 'images, pictures, imaginings' — their hearts generate endless schemes.

verses.text_hebrew: יָ֭מִיקוּ וִידַבְּר֣וּ בְרָ֑ע עֹ֝֗שֶׁק מִמָּר֥וֹם יְדַבֵּֽרוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They are corrupt, and speak wickedly concerning oppression: they speak loftily.
verses.rendering:
They mock and speak with malice;
they speak of oppression from on high.
translator_notes: The verb yamiku is difficult — it may mean 'they mock, they scoff' or 'they dissolve, they melt' (i.e., they make others dissolve with fear). The phrase mi-marom yedabberu ('they speak from on high') captures their arrogance: they speak as if from a throne, as if they are above everyone, as if their position exempts them from accountability.

verses.text_hebrew: שַׁתּ֣וּ בַשָּׁמַ֣יִם פִּיהֶ֑ם וּ֝לְשׁוֹנָ֗ם תִּהֲלַ֥ךְ בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: They set their mouth against the heavens, and their tongue walketh through the earth.
verses.rendering:
They set their mouths against the heavens,
and their tongues strut through the earth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The scope of the wicked's arrogance is total: their mouths reach to heaven (challenging God) and their tongues walk through the earth (dominating people). The image is of speech that has no boundaries — they blaspheme upward and oppress outward. The verb tihalakh ('it walks, it goes about') personifies the tongue as an independent agent, a roving force of destruction.
translator_notes: The phrase shattu vashamayim pihem ('they set their mouths in the heavens') is an image of cosmic arrogance — mouths positioned against the sky itself, speaking as rivals to God. This is not mere rudeness but theological rebellion.

verses.text_hebrew: לָ֭כֵן יָשׁ֣וּב עַמּ֣וֹ הֲלֹ֑ם וּמֵ֥י מָ֝לֵ֗א יִמָּ֥צוּ לָֽמוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Therefore his people return hither: and waters of a full cup are wrung out to them.
verses.rendering:
Therefore the people turn to them,
and waters of abundance are drained by them.
translator_notes: This difficult verse seems to describe the wicked's followers — ordinary people who observe their prosperity and are drawn to them. The 'waters of abundance' (mei male) likely means the wicked drink deeply of success, draining the cup of prosperity dry. The verse captures the social effect of visible wickedness: it attracts a following.

verses.text_hebrew: וְֽאָמְר֗וּ אֵיכָ֥ה יָדַֽע־אֵ֑ל וְיֵ֖שׁ דֵּעָ֣ה בְעֶלְיֽוֹן׃
verses.text_kjv: And they say, How doth God know? and is there knowledge in the most High?
verses.rendering:
And they say, 'How can God know?
Is there knowledge in the Most High?'
verses.expanded_rendering: The wicked now speak directly, and their words are a denial of divine omniscience. Eikhah yada El ('How can God know?') challenges God's awareness of human affairs, while ve-yesh de'ah be-Elyon ('is there knowledge in the Most High?') questions whether the supreme deity even has cognitive access to what happens on earth. This is practical atheism — not a philosophical denial of God's existence but a functional denial of His involvement. If God does not see, then there are no consequences.
translator_notes: The title Elyon ('Most High') emphasizes God's supreme position — and the irony is sharp: the very God whose exalted position should guarantee oversight is the one whose knowledge they deny. The higher God is, the less He sees — that is their theology.

verses.text_hebrew: הִנֵּה־אֵ֥לֶּה רְשָׁעִ֑ים וְשַׁלְוֵ֥י ע֝וֹלָ֗ם הִשְׂגּוּ־חָֽיִל׃
verses.text_kjv: Behold, these are the ungodly, who prosper in the world; they increase in riches.
verses.rendering:
Look — these are the wicked,
always at ease, piling up wealth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist steps back and summarizes the problem with devastating clarity: hinneh elleh resha'im ('Look — these are the wicked'). The demonstrative hinneh ('look, behold') is the psalmist pointing in astonishment. The word shalvei ('those at ease, tranquil ones') is from the same root as shalom — the wicked possess the very tranquility that should belong to the faithful. And they hisgu chayil ('increase wealth, gain power') — the rich get richer. This verse is the crisis condensed to a single observation.
translator_notes: The phrase shalvei olam can mean 'perpetually at ease' or 'at ease in the world.' Either reading conveys the same frustration: the wicked enjoy uninterrupted, seemingly permanent tranquility.

verses.text_hebrew: אַךְ־רִ֥יק זִכִּ֗יתִי לְבָ֫בִ֥י וָאֶרְחַ֥ץ בְּנִקָּי֗וֹן כַּפָּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Verily I have cleansed my heart in vain, and washed my hands in innocency.
verses.rendering:
Surely I have kept my heart pure for nothing
and washed my hands in innocence in vain.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the psalm's darkest verse. The word riq means 'emptiness, vanity, for nothing' — the psalmist's entire moral effort now appears pointless. Zikkiti levavi ('I have purified my heart') and erchats beniqayon kappai ('I have washed my hands in innocence') describe a life of deliberate, sustained moral discipline — and the word akh riq ('surely for nothing') demolishes it. If the wicked prosper and the righteous suffer, then righteousness is a bad investment. This is not a casual complaint; it is the logical conclusion of the evidence, and the psalmist has the honesty to state it.
translator_notes: The phrase 'washed my hands in innocence' echoes Psalm 26:6 and the ritual handwashing of innocence (cf. Deuteronomy 21:6). The psalmist has maintained both inner purity (heart) and outer conduct (hands) — and concludes it was worthless.

verses.text_hebrew: וָאֱהִ֣י נָ֭גוּעַ כׇּל־הַיּ֑וֹם וְ֝תוֹכַחְתִּ֗י לַבְּקָרֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: For all the day long have I been plagued, and chastened every morning.
verses.rendering:
I have been stricken all day long
and punished every morning.
translator_notes: The contrast with verses 4-5 is deliberate: the wicked have no pain (verse 4) and are not stricken like others (verse 5), but the psalmist is nagua ('stricken, plagued') kol hayyom ('all day long') and receives tokhachah ('punishment, correction') labbeqarot ('every morning'). Morning, which should bring fresh mercy, brings only fresh suffering.

verses.text_hebrew: אִם־אָ֭מַרְתִּי אֲסַפְּרָ֥ה כְמ֑וֹ הִנֵּ֤ה ד֖וֹר בָּנֶ֣יךָ בָגָֽדְתִּי׃
verses.text_kjv: If I say, I will speak thus; behold, I should offend against the generation of thy children.
verses.rendering:
If I had said, 'I will speak this way' —
I would have betrayed the generation of Your children.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist catches himself. He recognizes that voicing his doubts publicly would be an act of bagadti ('treachery, betrayal') against dor vanekha ('the generation of Your children') — the community of faith. This is a remarkable moment of self-awareness: the psalmist distinguishes between having doubts (which he cannot help) and broadcasting them (which would damage others). His silence is not dishonesty but pastoral responsibility.
translator_notes: The verb bagadti ('I would have betrayed, dealt treacherously') is a strong word — it is used for marital infidelity, treaty violations, and religious apostasy. The psalmist understands that publicizing his crisis of faith would be a form of violence against the believing community.

verses.text_hebrew: וָ֭אֲחַשְּׁבָה לָדַ֣עַת זֹ֑את עָמָ֖ל ה֣וּא בְעֵינָֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: When I thought to know this, it was too painful for me;
verses.rendering:
When I tried to understand this,
it was troubling in my eyes —
translator_notes: The word amal ('labor, trouble, toil') is the same word used in verse 5 for the suffering the wicked avoid. Now the psalmist's attempt to comprehend the problem is itself amal — even thinking about it is exhausting. The problem resists rational solution.

verses.text_hebrew: עַד־אָ֭בוֹא אֶל־מִקְדְּשֵׁי־אֵ֑ל אָ֝בִ֗ינָה לְאַחֲרִיתָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: Until I went into the sanctuary of God; then understood I their end.
verses.rendering:
until I entered the sanctuary of God;
then I perceived their end.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse is the psalm's turning point. The phrase ad avo el miqdeshei El ('until I entered the sanctuaries of God') marks a transition from rational analysis to experiential encounter. The psalmist does not solve the problem intellectually; he enters sacred space and there receives perception. The verb avinah ('I perceived, I discerned') is from bin, which means to discern, to distinguish, to see through — it implies insight rather than information. What does the psalmist perceive? Their acharit ('end, latter part, final outcome'). The wicked's story is not over; there is a final chapter that changes everything.
translator_notes: The plural miqdeshei ('sanctuaries') may refer to the temple complex with its multiple courts, or it may be a plural of majesty. The key point is that the resolution comes not from philosophical reasoning but from worship — from entering God's presence.

key_terms.semantic_range: end, latter part, final outcome, future, posterity, what comes after
key_terms.note: Acharit does not mean 'death' specifically but 'final outcome' — the end of the story, the last chapter, the ultimate destiny. The psalmist's crisis was caused by looking at the middle of the wicked's story; resolution comes from perceiving the ending.

verses.text_hebrew: אַ֣ךְ בַּ֭חֲלָקוֹת תָּשִׁ֣ית לָ֑מוֹ הִ֝פַּלְתָּ֗ם לְמַשּׁוּאֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: Surely thou didst set them in slippery places: thou castedst them down into destruction.
verses.rendering:
Surely You set them on slippery ground;
You cast them down to ruin.
verses.expanded_rendering: The irony is exact: in verse 2, the psalmist's feet nearly slipped; now it is the wicked who stand on chalaqot ('slippery places, smooth ground'). The appearance of stability was an illusion — the ground beneath the wicked was always treacherous. God did not need to intervene dramatically; He simply placed them where they would inevitably fall. The verb hippaltam ('You cast them down, You brought them down') and mashu'ot ('ruin, desolation') describe total collapse.
translator_notes: The word chalaqot ('slippery places') connects to the wisdom tradition's warnings about the 'slippery path' (Proverbs 5:6, Jeremiah 23:12). The wicked's prosperity was always precarious — the psalmist simply could not see it until he entered the sanctuary.

verses.text_hebrew: אֵ֤יךְ הָי֣וּ לְשַׁמָּ֣ה כְרָ֑גַע סָ֥פוּ תַ֝מּ֗וּ מִן־בַּלָּהֽוֹת׃
verses.text_kjv: How are they brought into desolation, as in a moment! they are utterly consumed with terrors.
verses.rendering:
How suddenly they become a horror!
They are swept away, utterly destroyed by terrors.
translator_notes: The word ke-raga ('like a moment, in an instant') emphasizes the speed of their collapse. The prosperity that seemed permanent vanishes instantaneously. The triple emphasis — shammah ('horror, desolation'), safu ('they are swept away'), tammu ('they are finished, consumed') — leaves nothing standing.

verses.text_hebrew: כַּחֲל֥וֹם מֵהָקִ֑יץ אֲ֝דֹנָ֗י בָּעִ֤יר ׀ צַלְמָ֬ם תִּבְזֶֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: As a dream when one awaketh; so, O Lord, when thou awakest, thou shalt despise their image.
verses.rendering:
Like a dream when one awakens —
O Lord, when You rouse Yourself,
You will despise their image.
verses.expanded_rendering: The wicked's existence is compared to a dream — vivid and convincing while it lasts, but dissolving instantly upon waking. The verb tivzeh ('You will despise, hold in contempt') and tsalmam ('their image, their form, their phantom') suggest that the wicked were never as substantial as they appeared. Their power was phantasmal — a projection, not a reality. When God 'rouses' (ba-ir, from ur, 'to awake'), the illusion vanishes.
translator_notes: The image of God awakening is anthropomorphic — God does not literally sleep — but it captures the psalmist's perception: the period of the wicked's prosperity felt like God was asleep, and their judgment will feel like God waking up. The same language appears in Psalm 44:24 ('Awake! Why do You sleep, O Lord?').

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֣י יִ֭תְחַמֵּץ לְבָבִ֑י וְ֝כִלְיוֹתַ֗י אֶשְׁתּוֹנָֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: Thus my heart was grieved, and I was pricked in my reins.
verses.rendering:
When my heart was embittered
and I was pierced in my innermost being,
translator_notes: The verb yitchammets ('was embittered, was soured') comes from chamets ('to be sour, to leaven') — the same root as vinegar. The psalmist's heart had gone sour with envy. The kilyot ('kidneys, innermost parts') were considered the seat of deep emotions and conscience in Hebrew anthropology. The psalmist's entire inner life had been corroded by the problem.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִי־בַ֥עַר וְלֹ֣א אֵדָ֑ע בְּ֝הֵמ֗וֹת הָיִ֥יתִי עִמָּֽךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: So foolish was I, and ignorant: I was as a beast before thee.
verses.rendering:
I was senseless and did not understand;
I was like a brute beast before You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist's self-assessment is brutally honest. The word ba'ar ('brutish, stupid, senseless') describes animal-like incomprehension — the inability to see beyond immediate appearances. The word behemot ('beasts, cattle') is the plural of behemah and is the same word used for the great beast in Job 40:15. The psalmist is saying: when I envied the wicked, I was thinking like an animal — reacting to what was in front of me without perceiving the larger reality. The phrase immakh ('before You, with You') makes it worse: he was this stupid in the very presence of God.
translator_notes: This verse is the psalm's act of repentance. The crisis of faith described in verses 2-16 is now diagnosed as a failure of perception — not a failure of God. The psalmist takes responsibility for his near-apostasy by calling it what it was: stupidity.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִ֣י תָמִ֣יד עִמָּ֑ךְ אָ֝חַ֗זְתָּ בְּיַד־יְמִינִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Nevertheless I am continually with thee: thou hast holden me by my right hand.
verses.rendering:
Yet I am always with You;
You hold my right hand.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word va-ani ('yet I, nevertheless I') marks the final turn. Despite his stupidity, despite his near-apostasy, despite everything — tamid immakh ('I am always with You'). The psalmist did not lose his relationship with God even when he nearly lost his faith. And then the stunning reversal: achazta beyad yemini ('You have grasped my right hand'). The psalmist did not hold on to God; God held on to him. The right hand is the hand of strength, of action, of oath-taking. God seized it and did not let go, even when the psalmist's feet were slipping.
translator_notes: The phrase 'You hold my right hand' reverses the image of verse 2 (feet slipping). The hand that was grasping at nothing is now held by God. This is one of the Psalter's most powerful statements of grace — God's grip is stronger than the psalmist's doubt.

verses.text_hebrew: בַּעֲצָתְךָ֥ תַנְחֵ֑נִי וְ֝אַחַ֗ר כָּב֥וֹד תִּקָּחֵֽנִי׃
verses.text_kjv: Thou shalt guide me with thy counsel, and afterward receive me to glory.
verses.rendering:
With Your counsel You guide me,
and afterward You will take me into glory.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase ba-atsatekha tancheni ('with Your counsel You guide me') means God's guidance is not merely external direction but intimate counsel — the word etsah implies strategic wisdom, a plan. The second line — ve-achar kavod tiqqacheni ('and afterward into glory You will take me') — is one of the most debated phrases in the Psalter. Does 'afterward' mean 'after death'? Does 'glory' mean heaven? The Hebrew is ambiguous. But the verb tiqqacheni ('You will take me') is the same verb used for Enoch's translation (Genesis 5:24: 'God took him') and Elijah's ascension (2 Kings 2:3, 5). At minimum, the psalmist believes God's care extends beyond the present crisis. At maximum, he is expressing hope for life beyond death.
translator_notes: The phrase achar kavod has been translated as 'afterward to glory,' 'after glory,' 'according to glory,' and 'with honor afterward.' The ambiguity may be intentional — the psalmist is reaching beyond what he can clearly articulate, groping toward a hope that the Hebrew Bible's theology of death has not yet fully developed.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, weight, honor, splendor, the manifest presence of God
key_terms.note: In this context, kavod may refer to God's glorious presence — the psalmist hopes to be taken into the sphere of God's own glory. Whether this implies a post-mortem hope or an intensified experience of God's presence in this life, the aspiration is for proximity to God Himself.

verses.text_hebrew: מִי־לִ֥י בַשָּׁמָ֑יִם וְ֝עִמְּךָ֗ לֹא־חָפַ֥צְתִּי בָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: Whom have I in heaven but thee? and there is none upon earth that I desire beside thee.
verses.rendering:
Whom have I in heaven but You?
And besides You I desire nothing on earth.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is arguably the most intimate verse in the entire Psalter. The rhetorical question mi li vashamayim ('who is there for me in heaven?') sweeps the entire celestial realm and finds no one and nothing comparable to God. The second line — ve-immekha lo chafatsti va-arets ('and with You I have no desire on earth') — does the same for the terrestrial realm. The psalmist has moved from envying the wicked's prosperity (verse 3) to declaring that he desires nothing on earth besides God. The entire crisis has been resolved not by an explanation of suffering but by a deepened experience of God. The answer to 'Why do the wicked prosper?' turns out to be: it does not matter, because God Himself is enough.
translator_notes: The verb chafats ('to desire, to delight in, to take pleasure in') is a strong word — it describes deep, passionate wanting. The psalmist is not saying he lacks earthly desires; he is saying that compared to God, no earthly desire registers. This is not asceticism but adoration.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּלָ֥ה שְׁאֵרִ֗י וּלְבָ֫בִ֥י צוּר־לְבָבִ֥י וְחֶלְקִ֗י אֱלֹהִ֥ים לְעוֹלָֽם׃
verses.text_kjv: My flesh and my heart faileth: but God is the strength of my heart, and my portion for ever.
verses.rendering:
My flesh and my heart may fail,
but God is the rock of my heart
and my portion forever.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalmist acknowledges what the enemies in Psalm 71 exploited: kalah she'eri ulevavi ('my flesh and my heart fail'). The body is declining, the heart is giving out. But the next phrase — tsur levavi vechelqi ('the rock of my heart and my portion') — declares that God is the bedrock beneath the failing flesh. The word tsur ('rock') is the most solid, immovable substance the psalmist knows. The word chelqi ('my portion, my inheritance, my lot') is the language of land distribution — each tribe received a portion of the promised land, but the Levites received God Himself as their portion (Numbers 18:20). The psalmist claims the Levitical inheritance: God is his share.
translator_notes: The parallel between failing flesh and enduring God creates a contrast between what is temporary and what is permanent. The psalmist does not deny mortality; he transcends it by anchoring his identity in something that cannot decay.

key_terms.semantic_range: portion, share, inheritance, allotted territory, lot in life
key_terms.note: In the tribal land distribution (Joshua 13-21), each tribe received a chelq — a portion of land. The Levites received no land; their portion was God Himself (Deuteronomy 10:9). To call God 'my portion' is to identify with the Levites and to declare that God's presence is more valuable than any material inheritance.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־הִנֵּ֣ה רְחֵקֶ֣יךָ יֹאבֵ֑דוּ הִ֝צְמַ֗תָּה כׇּל־זוֹנֶ֥ה מִמֶּֽךָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For, lo, they that are far from thee shall perish: thou hast destroyed all them that go a whoring from thee.
verses.rendering:
For those far from You will perish;
You destroy all who are unfaithful to You.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm now returns to the fate of the wicked, but with transformed perspective. The phrase rechaqekha ('those far from You') defines wickedness not as a moral category but as a relational one — distance from God. The word zoneh ('one who is unfaithful, who whores') uses the prophetic marriage metaphor: unfaithfulness to God is adultery. The psalmist now sees that proximity to God is life and distance from God is death — and this realization makes the wicked's prosperity irrelevant.
translator_notes: The verb hitsmattah ('You have destroyed, cut off, silenced') is from tsamat, meaning to put an end to something decisively. The language of sexual unfaithfulness (zoneh) for spiritual apostasy is characteristic of Hosea and Ezekiel but appears here in the Psalms as well.

verses.text_hebrew: וַאֲנִ֤י ׀ קִרְבַ֥ת אֱלֹהִ֗ים לִ֫י ט֥וֹב שַׁתִּ֤י ׀ בַּאדֹנָ֣י יְהוִה֣ מַחְסִ֑י לְ֝סַפֵּ֗ר כׇּל־מַלְאֲכוֹתֶֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: But it is good for me to draw near to God: I have put my trust in the Lord GOD, that I may declare all thy works.
verses.rendering:
But as for me — nearness to God is my good.
I have made the Lord GOD my refuge,
so that I may tell of all Your works.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm ends where Psalm 71 began — with refuge. The psalmist's final declaration is qirvat Elohim li tov ('the nearness of God is my good'). This is the answer to the entire crisis: not an explanation of why the wicked prosper, not a timetable for their destruction, but a discovery that proximity to God is itself the highest good. The word qirvat ('nearness, closeness') is the opposite of the rechaqekha ('those far from You') in verse 27. The wicked are far and perish; the psalmist is near and lives. The final purpose clause — lesapper kol mal'akhotekha ('to tell all Your works') — transforms the psalmist from a doubter into a witness. His near-apostasy has become testimony material.
translator_notes: The closing phrase echoes Psalm 71:17-18 — the psalmist will declare God's works to the next generation. The crisis of faith has not destroyed his witness; it has deepened it. He now has something to testify about that he did not have before: how God held him when his feet were slipping.
